perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach by Vendor of the confidentiality requirements of this Agreement, the University shall have the right to seek injunctive and/or other preliminary or equitable relief, without having to prove actual damages or to post a bond. Such relief shall be in addition to any other remedies and damages available to the University.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a breach by Vendor of the confidentiality requirements of this Agreement, the University shall have the right to seek injunctive and/or other preliminary or equitable relief, provided that the University posts a bond and proves actual damages. Such relief shall be in addition to any other remedies and damages available to the University.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds the requirement for the University to post a bond and prove actual damages before seeking injunctive relief. This could contradict Ohio law, which may allow for injunctive relief without these requirements in certain circumstances, hindering the University's ability to quickly protect its confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 2727.02 outlines grounds for injunction. By requiring the University to post a bond and prove actual damages, the modified text imposes additional hurdles beyond what Ohio law may require for obtaining an injunction to protect trade secrets or confidential information. The law permits injunctions under certain conditions without explicitly mandating a bond or proof of actual damages in all cases. Therefore, the contract becomes more restrictive than the underlying Ohio law, potentially impairing the University's legal rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 2727.02 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 2727.02 | Causes for an injunction. Ohio Revised Code / Title 27 Courts-General Provisions-Special Remedies / Chapter 2727 Injunctions Effective: October 1, 1953 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF A temporary order may be granted restraining an act when it appears by the petition that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and such relief, or any part of it, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of such act, the commission or continuance of which, during the litigation, would produce great or irreparable injury to the plaintiff, or when, during the litigation, it appears that the defendant is doing, threatens or is about to do, or is procuring or permitting to be done, such act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1953 – House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1953 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces requirements (posting a bond and proving actual damages) for the University to seek injunctive relief that are not explicitly mandated by Ohio Revised Code § 2727.02. The original text aligns with the statute, allowing the University to seek injunctive relief without those burdens. The added requirements make the contract more restrictive than the law, potentially impairing the University's ability to protect its confidential information, which contradicts the intent of the law to provide injunctive relief under certain conditions as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'A temporary order may be granted restraining an act when it appears by the petition that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded...'. By adding the bond and damages requirements, the contract could prevent the University from obtaining relief to which it would otherwise be entitled.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall not disclose to any other person, firm or corporation, other than its employees, directors, agents, or representatives, including without limitation, attorneys, accountants , appraisers and financial advisors, who have a “need to know” for purposes of completing any assessment of the Project (collectively, “representatives”) any of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party hereunder, by using the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information as Recipient employs with respect to its own proprieta ry and confidential information of like kind.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall not disclose to any other person, firm or corporation, other than its employees who have a “need to know” for purposes of completing any assessment of the Project any of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party hereunder, by using a reasonable degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the exception to *only* employees and removing the phrase "same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information as Recipient employs with respect to its own proprietary and confidential information of like kind" and replacing it with "reasonable degree of care", the clause effectively lowers the standard of care required to protect the Confidential Information. This weakens the protection of confidential information and potentially conflicts with Ohio law regarding trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61(B) defines misappropriation of a trade secret to include disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire the trade secret. By lowering the standard of care to “reasonable” instead of maintaining protections equivalent to the Recipient's own confidential information, the change increases the risk of misappropriation. This runs contrary to the purpose of Ohio's trade secret laws, which seeks to protect valuable business information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The changed text lowers the standard of care for protecting confidential information from 'same degree of care' to 'reasonable degree of care,' and limits disclosure exceptions. Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61(B) addresses misappropriation of trade secrets, including disclosure without consent. The 'reasonable degree of care' standard weakens the protection and increases the risk of misappropriation, contradicting the law's intent to safeguard trade secrets. Snippet 1 defines misappropriation as disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, highlighting the legal concern that the revised clause would likely fail to adequately protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: If any court or regulatory order or other service of legal process or open records statute requires the Recipient to disclose information covered by its confidentiality obligation, then such party will provide the Disclosing Party with prompt notice of any such order or process so that the Disc losing Party may attempt to prev ent such order of disclosure, and the parties shall also work together to ensure that any such required disclosure is carried out pursuant to a protective order (or other similar protection) to prevent further disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: If any court or regulatory order or other service of legal process or open records statute requires the Recipient to disclose information covered by its confidentiality obligation, the Recipient will comply with the order.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for the Recipient to notify the Disclosing Party of any legal process requiring disclosure of confidential information. It also removes the obligation to work together to obtain a protective order. This could violate Ohio law, which favors cooperation in protecting trade secrets and confidential information, and may require reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality even when disclosure is legally compelled.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Rule of Civil Procedure 26(C) allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information in discovery. By removing the requirement for the Recipient to notify the Disclosing Party and cooperate in obtaining a protective order, the change hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information during litigation or other legal proceedings. While the Recipient is still required to comply with the order, the omission prevents the Disclosing party from preventing or limiting the disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Recipient to notify the Disclosing Party of legal processes requiring disclosure and to cooperate in obtaining a protective order. The changed text removes these obligations, requiring only compliance with the order. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation highlight that this hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Rule 26(C) (even though not explicitly mentioned in the snippet), supports the ability of a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information in discovery, which the modified text undermines by removing the notification and cooperation requirements.
